I just finished watching the film. I'm not going to spoil it. Then Mr Han traveled back to New York after leaving Larusso's house. It's like Mr Han took a taxi to go from NY to CA in 1 day. I don't know did Mr Han took the plane to travel back and forth from NY to CA. It would make sense. You know what doesn't make sense? The pizza was delivered from NY to CA. How is it possible? I won't tell you which scene. Once you watch it, you'll see a plot hole. It's like people wondered how Bruce Wayne got back in Gotham City after escaping the Pit in Dark Knight Rises.

But can we change the name of the movie. Because  it's  not karate it's Kung Fu.
I understand white people has this thing with Asians with pretty much anything (insert any type of asian here) but that isn't how it works.
We have our own cultures, traditions and beliefs. 
Even our martial arts doesn't come from the same place or for the same reason.
For example: Karate
Was invented by Okinawans to defend themselves from mainland Japan and because Okinawans (my people the real Japanese lol)
Are commoners they wasn't allowed to have Katana's. 
So they invented karate if you even look at most of the weapons they used you will see it's  all farming tools. Sai's to move hay, Kama to cut grain, bow staff to beat the grain, etc.

Kung Fun on the other hand was more based on Confucianism and Buddhism. 
And depending on the types of Kung Fu that to comes from different things.

Just like how Muay Thai came about on it's  own.

Alright.  SPOILERS BELLOW. IF YOU DON'T WANT SPOILERS,  SKIP THIS COMMENT.  YOU HAVE BEEN WARNED. 













the movie is very short,  around 90 minutes,  which is a shame.  They only bring Daniel in 1h into the movie.  The first hour or so is all about the boy and kung fu, and how he adapts in NY. He even trains a guy to mix boxing with kung fu (wtf). The villain is not developed at all,  nor his karate teacher.  He is just plain bad,  because.... then they bring in daniel,  only to have him compete with mr han. I don't know why mr han seeks daniel's help and the,  when daniel comes,  both compete with each other,  saying that kung-fu is better then karate and vice versa.  The training is mostly fast forwarded and the tournament is almost entirely skipped.  It seems like they ran out of time trying to keep the movie down to 90 min.  The results are predictable (as all karate kid movies). At least in the final scene we have comic relief with the appearance of Johnny Lawrence. To me,  this is inferior to the 2010 movie,  even if the acting here and the production are superior.  It is a 6/10 for me.  High points go for the acting,  which is very good.  This movie would have benefited from an extra 30 min in runtime.

Kukg fu is really good for keeping your body flexible, karate is really good for specific moves good for specific situations, boxing is good for building stamina with your punches so you don't get tired, etc. Obviously this movie is only combining kung fu and karate, but it hits the same fundamental idea, especially at 1:50 when they say, "You have a foundation of kung fu; we're going to build upon that with karate."

🥲It’s a bit heartbreaking to see our childhood hero, Jackie Chan, getting older (We are also getting older as well). Jackie wasn’t just an action star — he was a symbol of dedication, fearlessness, and creativity. His breathtaking stunts, most of which he performed himself, left us on the edge of our seats, wondering how one man could blend comedy, martial arts, and danger so effortlessly.. ✨

Jackie didn’t just entertain — he inspired. His movies weren’t just about punches and kicks; they were about perseverance, quick thinking, and finding humor even in the most intense situations. Classics like Drunken Master, Police Story, Rush Hour, and Project A weren’t just blockbusters — they became part of our memories..

He broke bones, got injured countless times, yet always came back with a smile — reminding us that passion and hard work can overcome anything.. 😱

Though time moves on, Jackie Chan's legacy is timeless. He may age, but the hero he’s been for us — the man who made us laugh, cheer, and sometimes hold our breath — will forever remain young in our hearts...❤️❤️❤️

Ralph Macchio desperately wants to reestablish Daniel LaRusso as a major lead character and break away from the Cobra Kai series where he was relegated to a supporting role.
Jackie Chan desperately seeks to escape the fact that he was overshadowed by Michelle Yeoh's Oscar win for Everything Everywhere All At Once.

So freakin excited for this movie! 🖤Anyone who's an oldschool martial arts movies fan, here is an interesting easter egg I myself just noticed going back and  rewatching the three original Karate Kid movies after two decades.. If you have ever seen Bruce Lee's Way of the Dragon, pay attention to how he fights - esp. the fight against Chuck Norris. He does some of the same moves that Daniel does in Karate Kid. You can see him use blocking and intercepting defensive techniques similar to Karate Kid's "wax on/wax off", the drum technique (den-den daiko) that Daniel used in Japan for his fight against Chozen in KK2, and the "scissor arm, knee pop" move that paralyzes the muscle (Chuck's arm and leg) we also see used by Daniel in Cobra Kai season 3, episode 5.  That is because Pat E. Johnson, who choreographed the classic Okinawan style fight scenes in The Karate Kid, had previously worked with both Bruce Lee and Chuck Norris.

Muay Thai means 8 extremities fist 

The Kung Fu style Baji Quan also means 8 Extremities fist 

Kung Fu means hard work

Karate means empty hand

Gichin Funikoshi, the founder of Shotokan Karate, coined the term "Karate" back in the 1920's when he moved from  Okinawa to Japan because he knew "Chinese Hand" wouldn't sell in Japan 

Originally in Okinawa Karate was called Tou De which means Chinese Hand 

Tou De's main two influences as the Kung Fu style Xing Yi Quan which means True Form Fist, and Southern White Crane Kung Fu 

This is because China was friendly Okinawa for many years due to fishing and business trade relations vsoctjey send over scholars to teach the royalty and elite who's then pass it down to the townsfolk.

Alongside Shotokan Karate, so was Ishin-Ryu, Shito-Ryu, and Goju-Ryu poached from Okinawa to come over to Japan.

The founder of Goju-Ryu was Chojun Miyagi 


Goju-Ryu means hard soft style 
because it merged the hard Northen linear style of Xing Yi Quan and the soft circular style of Southern White Crane 

One of Chojun Miyagi's students eventually moved to New York 

One of that student's students in NY was Robert Mark Kamen who grew up to be a writer and wrote the Karate Kid

Robert based Mr Miyagi loosely on his instructor and named his character Mr Miyagi as tribute to Chojun Miyagi.

The story of Karate Kid was loosely based on Robert as he was bullied and beat up at the World's Fair in Queens NY when he was young and then tried to join a martial arts school in Manhattan but quit because of its controversial strict teachings and he didn't like the head instructor.

Then he met the student of Chojun Miyagi who was teaching privately.

The fight choreographer for Karate Kid movie was Pat Johnson, a student of Chuck Norris 

Chuck Norris was in the military and studied Tang Soo Do from a Korean master

Tang Soo Do means Way of the Chinese Hand because one of its founders studied Chinese martial arts in China during Japan's occupation of Korea which had banned the practice of martial arts 

The other founder, Won Kuk Lee, because of the ban, moved over to Japan to attend college at Chou University and trained under Gichin Funikoshi at his nearby gym for over 20 years until moving back to Korea to help create Tang Soo Do 


As such, the fictional school Cobra Kai, was based on Tang Soo Do, and they created the storyline that John Kreese and Terry Silver trained in Tang Soo Do during the war and brought back what they learned in the states.

And Mr Miyagi use the symbol of the crane because that's a nod to Southern White Crane and also the Okinawan crushing trade with China 

In the Karate Kid remake (also known as Kung Fu Dream in China), when Dre Parker aka Will Smith's troubled son Jaden Smith, is observing a woman doing a Kung fu form by a temple, she is performing Southern White Crane.

The Crane kick, while fictional is a nod to the style as well.

I'd like to think during the big fight, our young hero is being bested by his opponent after a very impressive start.  It's down to the wire, and our young hero feels he maybe bit off a bit more than he can chew.  That his opponent IS the better fighter.  With the feeling of defeat washing over his face, his resolve settles in and he's about to call it quits.  He looks over to his opponent's side and sees them laughing.  Then, from the other side of the mat he hears "Hey kid, sweep the leg!" and sees Johnny's hands up to his mouth yelling over the sounds of the audience.  Miguel is next to him, nodding in agreement.  The "sweep the leg" comment, not literally being about sweeping a leg, but instead a call to arms:  NOW it's time to bring out your best you.  To fight, with mind, body and spirit.  Now, it's time.   Our young hero now stands up, ready to fight.  But this time, he is reminded he isn't fighting alone.  He's fighting with the entire Miyagi lineage behind him.
